Debt collectors calling constantly, at work, or harassing you? This violates federal law. Learn how to stop it.

Collections Calling Too Much: Medical Debt Collector Harassment Violations

Understanding Your Situation

A medical debt collector is calling you repeatedly—multiple times per day, early morning, late night, at work, or using abusive language. You've asked them to stop, but they continue. You feel harassed, stressed, and powerless. This is illegal harassment under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA, 15 U.S.C. § 1692d). Federal law strictly prohibits debt collectors from using harassment, abuse, or oppressive tactics to collect debts. Excessive calls, calls at unreasonable hours, calls to your employer, threats, profanity, and intimidation are all violations. You have powerful legal rights to stop this harassment and potentially sue collectors for damages.

Why This Happens

Aggressive Collection Tactics: Many collection agencies use high-volume calling as a primary collection strategy, often calling the same debtor dozens of times per week.

Quota-Based Compensation: Collection agents are often paid based on collections made, incentivizing aggressive and sometimes illegal tactics.

Lack of Regulation Enforcement: Many small or overseas collection agencies operate with little oversight, routinely violating FDCPA.

Automated Dialing Systems: Collectors use auto-dialers that may call multiple times per day without human oversight of frequency.

Multiple Accounts/Agencies: If you have multiple medical debts, multiple agencies may be calling you, compounding the harassment.

Ignoring Consumer Requests: Collectors may ignore your requests to stop calling or call at different times, hoping to wear you down.

Intentional Intimidation: Some collectors intentionally harass debtors to pressure them into immediate payment, even using threats or abusive language.

What To Check

✓ Document all calls: Log every call—date, time, phone number, what was said, who called
✓ Check call frequency: How many times per day/week are they calling? Multiple calls per day is likely excessive
✓ Note call times: Are they calling before 8 AM or after 9 PM? (FDCPA violation)
✓ Identify workplace calls: Are they calling your workplace after you told them not to? (FDCPA violation)
✓ Document abusive language: Have they used profanity, threats, or intimidation?
✓ Record threats: Have they threatened arrest, lawsuit, wage garnishment, or legal action they can't actually take?
✓ Check for third-party disclosures: Have they discussed your debt with family, neighbors, coworkers, or others?
✓ Verify identity: Is it actually a legitimate debt collector or a scam?
✓ Save voicemails: Keep all voicemail messages as evidence

What To Do Next

Step 1: Send Written Cease Communication Letter
Under FDCPA § 1692c, you have the right to demand collectors stop all communication. Send via certified mail:

'This is a formal notice under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (15 U.S.C. § 1692c) demanding that you cease all communication with me regarding the debt [account number]. You are prohibited from contacting me by phone, mail, email, text, or any other means, except to inform me of specific legal actions you are taking (such as filing a lawsuit). Any further contact after receipt of this letter will constitute willful violation of the FDCPA and will result in legal action.'

Use our Medical Collections Debt Validation tool to generate a cease communication letter.

Note: This stops contact but doesn't make the debt go away—the collector can still sue you. However, it immediately stops harassment.

Step 2: Document All FDCPA Violations
Create comprehensive documentation:
• Call log: Date, time, duration of every call
• Phone records: Screenshots of call history from your phone
• Voicemails: Save all voicemails; note threats or abusive language
• Witness statements: If others heard abusive calls or threats
• Workplace issues: Document if employer complained or you faced work consequences
• Emotional distress: Note anxiety, sleep disruption, stress from harassment

This documentation supports potential legal action.

Step 3: Assert Your Rights During Calls
If you choose to answer calls before sending cease letter, clearly state:

'I am recording this call for quality assurance. You are calling me [X times per day / at unreasonable hours / at my workplace after I told you not to]. This constitutes harassment under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. I demand you only call [specify: once per week on weekdays between 10 AM - 5 PM / never call at work / only contact me in writing]. If you continue harassing me, I will file complaints and pursue legal action.'

Step 4: Check State Call Limits and Recording Laws
Some states have laws stricter than FDCPA regarding collection calls:
• Maximum call frequency (e.g., once per week)
• Prohibited contact methods
• Required disclosures

Also check if your state allows one-party recording of phone calls (most states do). If allowed, record all collection calls as evidence.

Step 5: File Complaints with Regulators
File complaints with:

• Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB): File CFPB Complaint
• Federal Trade Commission (FTC): Report to FTC
• Your State Attorney General: Consumer protection division
• Better Business Bureau: Lodge complaint against collection agency

Include your detailed call log and description of harassment in all complaints.

Step 6: Block the Numbers
While pursuing legal remedies:
• Block all known collector phone numbers on your phone
• Register with National Do Not Call Registry (though debt collectors are exempt, it helps document your wishes)
• Use call-blocking apps to filter unknown numbers

Step 7: Consider Legal Action
If harassment continues or was severe, consult a consumer rights attorney specializing in FDCPA:

• You can sue for actual damages (emotional distress, lost wages, medical bills from stress)
• You can recover statutory damages up to $1,000 per lawsuit
• Collector must pay your attorney fees and costs if you win
• You have one year from the violation date to file

Many attorneys take FDCPA cases on contingency (free unless you win).

FDCPA harassment violations often result in settlements or judgments in favor of consumers.

Step 8: Negotiate to Stop Harassment
If you can afford to negotiate the debt, you can potentially stop harassment by:
• Requesting validation to pause collection
• Negotiating settlement with agreement to stop calls during negotiations
• Setting up payment plan with specific communication terms

However, don't let harassment pressure you into paying a debt you dispute or can't afford. Stop the harassment first, then deal with the debt on your terms.

For more guidance on medical debt and collections, see our Medical Debt Hub.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many collection calls per day is considered harassment?

The FDCPA doesn't specify an exact number, but courts have found that multiple calls per day (especially 5+) without a reasonable purpose constitutes harassment. Even a few calls per day may be harassment if they're at unreasonable hours, you've asked them to stop, or they're combined with threats or abuse. Document all call frequency to support your case.

Can debt collectors call me at work?

Collectors can call you at work unless you tell them your employer prohibits such calls or you otherwise request they not call at work. Once you make this request (preferably in writing), they must stop calling your workplace. Continued calls after notification is an FDCPA violation.

What times can debt collectors legally call?

Under FDCPA § 1692c(a)(1), collectors cannot call before 8:00 AM or after 9:00 PM in your time zone. Calls outside these hours are presumed to be harassment and are violations. Keep logs of any calls outside these hours.

Can I sue a debt collector for harassment?

Yes. FDCPA allows you to sue collectors for harassment and violations within one year of the violation. You can recover actual damages (including emotional distress), statutory damages up to $1,000, and attorney fees. Many consumer rights attorneys take these cases on contingency. Documented harassment violations often result in settlements or awards.

Will sending a cease communication letter make them sue me faster?

Possibly, but it immediately stops harassment, which is often worth the tradeoff. Many collection agencies don't sue because it's expensive and time-consuming. If they do sue, you have rights to defend the lawsuit. The cease letter doesn't admit you owe the debt—it just stops communication. You can still dispute the debt and negotiate if they initiate legal action.

Educational purposes only. This information is not legal, medical, or financial advice. FDCPA rights and harassment claims are complex. Consult a consumer rights attorney for personalized legal guidance.
